title: Refactor Planner
description: Use when planning a risky refactor, migration, decomposition, dependency untangling, architecture cleanup, codemod campaign, framework move, data migration, or public contract change that needs scope classification, pattern comparison, validation gates, and rollback criteria.

input_schema.current_code_summary: string — architecture pattern, LOC estimate, module dependencies, tech debt, pain points (e.g. 'Monolithic 50K LOC Node.js service, tight auth/storage coupling, 35% test coverage')
input_schema.target_state: string — desired structure, module boundaries, acceptance metrics (e.g. '@org/auth, @org/core, @org/storage with <10K LOC each, independent deploy')

instructions: Classify scope as Local, Module, Cross-module, Public contract, Data/runtime, or Platform/org; plan for the highest-risk scope when multiple apply.

Classify refactor type as local cleanup, boundary extraction, dependency inversion, subsystem decomposition, public contract migration, data/runtime migration, framework/platform migration, or mechanical codemod.

Compare at least two viable patterns such as characterize-then-change, extract interface, branch by abstraction, strangler fig, dual-read/write, shadow traffic, codemod campaign, compatibility shim, facade extraction, or vertical slice pilot.
Choose one pattern and reject weaker options with concrete risk reasons.

Define invariant behavior that must not change, including APIs, data contracts, persistence semantics, performance budgets, security boundaries, user workflows, observability, and compatibility promises.
Map dependencies and risk across callers, owners, transitive imports, side effects, persistence, caches, background jobs, deployments, feature flags, and release cadence.
Define phases with entry criteria, implementation scope, owned files/modules/services, exit criteria, validation commands or production signals, rollback path, and expected duration.
Specify required test coverage before each risky move, such as characterization, contract, migration, performance, data, compatibility, and smoke tests.
Include compatibility strategy for versioned interfaces, temporary shims, dual-write/read, shadow traffic, backfill, migration windows, and deprecation timing.
State stop conditions such as error budget burn, latency regression, data mismatch, test coverage gap, unclear owner, or compatibility break.
Do not recommend a rewrite without explaining why incremental migration is insufficient.

examples.input: Monolithic 50K LOC Node.js service with tightly coupled auth/business/storage. Target: modular packages with independent deploy.
examples.output:
Scope Classification: Cross-module decomposition with public-ish internal contracts. Highest risk is callers depending on auth/storage side effects.
Pattern Comparison: Extract interface fits because it stabilizes auth/storage boundaries before code moves; big-bang package split is rejected because caller and deploy regressions would be hard to isolate; strangler by use case is reserved for later storage routing.
Phase 1 (Characterization and boundary inventory, 1 week): Map auth/business/storage callers and side effects. Add characterization tests for login, permission checks, token refresh, and storage writes. Exit: top 20 auth/storage call paths covered; no behavior changes. Rollback: revert tests/inventory only.
Phase 2 (Interface extraction, 1-2 weeks): Define AuthProvider and StorageGateway contracts; adapt legacy implementation behind interfaces. Exit: contract tests pass against legacy implementation; package imports only depend on interfaces. Rollback: legacy concrete imports remain available behind a flag.
Phase 3 (Dual-write validation, 2-3 weeks): Run new storage in shadow mode for selected write paths. Exit: 0 critical discrepancies and <0.1% non-critical mismatch over 48h representative traffic. Rollback: disable dual-write flag.
Phase 4 (Adapter swap, 1 week): Route selected workflows to new storage. Exit: error rate <0.1% increase, p99 unchanged, rollback tested in staging. Rollback: revert routing flag within 5 minutes.
